text: During the emergency exploration, the surgeon performs a perineal examination revealing pus in the anal canal and observes that the visible length of the colon has completely sloughed out.
During laparoscopic abdominal inspection, the surgeon observes pus and slough in the pelvis, along with dense inflammatory adhesions between the bladder and the colonic conduit.

The surgeon carefully releases dense inflammatory adhesions between the bladder and the colonic conduit using suction and continuous irrigation, ensuring to avoid bladder injuries by staying close to the pericolic fat and the sigmoid wall.
The surgeon completes the pelvic dissection and focuses on separating the mesentery of the left colon from the retroperitoneum, carefully maneuvering to avoid damaging surrounding structures.
The surgeon continues dissection in the retroperitoneal plane, ensuring the separation of the mesentery from retroperitoneal structures without causing injury.
The surgeon connects the retroperitoneal and pelvic dissections, delivering the ischemic colon into the abdominal cavity for examination.
The surgeon visually assesses the line of demarcation on the colon, indicating the boundary between healthy and ischemic tissue.
The surgeon thoroughly irrigates the pelvic cavity and assesses the need for additional mobilization of the splenic flexure to facilitate the delivery of the colonic conduit from the anal canal.
The surgeon gently dissects the small bowel away from the inferior mesenteric artery clips and the retroperitoneum, using irrigation to assist in the separation process.
The surgeon approaches the splenic flexure from the inframesocolic and suprapancreatic sides, performing retroperitoneal dissection cranially and over the body of the pancreas.
The surgeon enters the lesser sac and divides the intervening tissue at the root of the transverse mesocolon, creating a clear separation in the surgical field.
The surgeon approaches the splenic flexure from the lateral side, continuing the mobilization process.
The surgeon divides the omental attachments to the left and transverse colon, reentering the lesser sac from below, which is visible as the tissue layers are separated.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the splenic flexure and transverse colon, ensuring these sections are fully freed from surrounding attachments.
The surgeon delivers the mobilized colon into the pelvis and out through the anal canal, completing the resection and extraction process.
The surgeon checks the orientation of the colon to ensure there is no twist in the mesentery, visually confirming the alignment of the colon and mesentery.
The surgeon positions the small bowel over the colonic mesentery to prevent internal herniation, ensuring proper alignment and avoiding potential complications.
The surgeon irrigates the pelvis thoroughly and inspects the bladder for any visible leaks following the repeat mobilization of the colon.
Under regional anesthesia, the surgeon performs a redo coloanal anastomosis, revising the colonic conduit after confirming its health and reduced inflammation, with no purulent discharge visible.
The final view shows the anal canal with good tone, indicating successful restoration of function.
